content.text: Adding an AI feature to a product raises an uncomfortable question that most product teams delay answering: does the feature make money or erode margin? When AI token cost is invisible to the product P&L and is simply absorbed into a shared infrastructure budget, the margin impact of every new AI capability is unknown until year-end when finance runs the numbers. Margin analysis for AI-powered features connects token cost to revenue at the feature level so product and engineering teams can make explicit decisions about which AI features are worth their cost — and at what price point.

attrs.title: Cost stack showing AI token cost as share of revenue and margin impact

content.text: AI cost as a component of gross margin

Gross margin is revenue minus cost of goods sold (COGS). For a SaaS product, COGS includes hosting, data transfer, third-party APIs, payment processing, and — increasingly — AI inference costs. The question margin analysis answers is: what is the gross margin on the AI-powered version of the product, and how does it compare to the non-AI version or to the target margin?
Cost component
Typical % of revenue (SaaS)
Notes
AI token cost
Varies widely by workload complexity and model tier; highest variance component
Infrastructure (hosting, compute)
Relatively stable; scales with user count
Third-party APIs and data
Stable; predictable per-unit cost
Support and customer success
Can decrease if AI reduces support volume
Payment processing
Fixed percentage of revenue
Target gross margin
AI at the higher end of token cost can compress this to 55–65%

The 60–80% gross margin target typical of SaaS businesses leaves 20–40% for COGS. When AI token cost alone consumes 20–25% of revenue, it crowds out other COGS components and leaves insufficient margin for operating expenses. The target for AI token cost is typically 10–20% of revenue, calibrated against the feature's contribution to conversion, retention, or willingness-to-pay uplift.
Isolating AI feature contribution margin

Contribution margin for an AI feature is the revenue attributable to that feature minus the incremental costs it introduces. For an AI writing assistant added to an existing product, the question is: what revenue would be lost if the feature were removed, and what costs would go away?
Revenue attribution methods

Attribution — determining how much revenue is 'caused by' the AI feature — is the most contested part of margin analysis. There is no perfectly accurate method; the goal is a defensible approximation that the business can use to make go/no-go decisions.
Margin sensitivity to model tier selection

The model tier chosen for an AI feature directly determines the floor on gross margin. A feature generating $10 of attributed revenue per user per month that runs on a model costing $3 per user starts at 30% AI cost — before adding infrastructure or support. Downgrading to a model that delivers the same quality at $1 per user reduces AI cost to 10%, restoring 20 percentage points of margin.

Running this analysis before selecting a model — not after observing unexpectedly low margins — is the right sequence. The quality score from the evals suite provides the quality constraint: select the cheapest model whose quality score exceeds the feature's minimum acceptable threshold.
Margin impact of usage concentration and throttling

In any user population, token consumption follows a power-law distribution: a small fraction of users account for a disproportionate share of token cost. When these heavy users are on a flat-rate subscription, they are subsidised by light users — the heavy users drive down the average margin to potentially unprofitable levels.

Measure usage concentration: calculate the share of total token cost generated by the top 5% and top 20% of users by consumption. If the top 5% drive more than 40% of AI cost, the pricing model has a concentration risk.

Implement soft usage quotas: set a monthly AI usage quota per user (e.g., 100 conversations) above which usage is rate-limited or requires an upsell to a higher tier. This protects margin without hard-blocking users.
Analyse P99 user cost: a P99 monthly token cost per user of $45 on a $29 subscription means the top 1% of users are unprofitable on the AI dimension — prioritise them for tier upsell.
Model the financial impact before enforcing quotas: compute how margin improves if the top 10% of heavy users are moved to a usage-based tier or their consumption is capped at the 90th percentile.
Building a margin model in a spreadsheet-free world

Margin analysis at the feature level should be automated, not a quarterly spreadsheet exercise. When token cost data flows into the data warehouse in near-real-time alongside revenue data, contribution margin per feature can be computed continuously and published on the FinOps dashboard.

Feeding this ledger from the cost allocation pipeline (which already has token costs by feature) and the revenue attribution model (which already has revenue by feature) requires only a join on feature_id and period. The FinOps dashboard then displays per-feature margin trends without manual intervention.
Common attribution approaches

A/B test attribution: the gold standard. Run a controlled experiment with and without the AI feature; the revenue lift in the treatment group is directly attributable to AI. Requires sufficient traffic and a long enough test window.

Cohort conversion attribution: compare conversion rate, upgrade rate, and churn rate for users who adopt the AI feature versus those who do not. The revenue difference in cohort outcomes is attributed to the feature.
Willingness-to-pay survey: survey users on whether they would pay a specific premium for the AI feature; use the response distribution to estimate the revenue the feature adds to average deal size.
Feature-gated pricing: offer the AI feature as a paid add-on or premium tier. Revenue from the add-on is directly attributable; no survey or inference is required.
Retention-based attribution: calculate the reduction in churn rate for AI-feature users vs. non-users and multiply the difference by average customer lifetime value.

When none of these methods is feasible (e.g., the AI feature is deeply integrated and cannot be A/B tested), use a conservative assumption — 10–15% of total revenue attributed to AI features — and disclose the assumption clearly in the analysis.
Pricing AI features to protect margin

If the AI feature's contribution margin analysis shows that token costs are compressing gross margin below target, the product team has three levers: reduce token cost, reduce usage, or raise price. Raising price is often the fastest lever and the one most aligned with delivering genuine value — if the feature is worth the cost, users should pay for it.
Pricing model
AI cost recovery mechanism
Risk
Flat subscription with AI included
Cost absorbed across all users; cross-subsidy from low-usage users
Heavy users can make the feature unprofitable at scale
Usage-based pricing per AI action
Direct cost pass-through; margin is stable regardless of usage
Complex pricing is harder to sell; usage friction may reduce adoption
AI feature as paid premium tier
Incremental revenue from upsell; margin on AI tier is explicit
Some users resist paying for AI; requires clear value demonstration
AI credits/quota system
Included credits cover expected usage; overage priced per credit
Requires operational credit management; user friction at overage

The usage-based or credit-based models are the most margin-safe for agentic features where cost per conversation is high and highly variable. Flat-rate models work well when cost per conversation is both low and predictable.
Break-even analysis: at what price is AI sustainable?

The break-even price per user is the minimum subscription price at which the AI feature earns zero contribution margin — all incremental AI costs are exactly covered by attributed revenue. Computing the break-even helps product teams know the floor below which the AI feature is a margin liability.

@dataclass
class AIBreakEvenAnalysis:
    users: int
    monthly_token_cost_usd: float
    monthly_ai_infra_usd: float
    monthly_ai_support_usd: float
    revenue_attribution_pct: float     # fraction of subscription credited to AI

    @property
    def total_ai_monthly_cost(self) -> float:
        return (self.monthly_token_cost_usd
                + self.monthly_ai_infra_usd
                + self.monthly_ai_support_usd)

    def break_even_price_per_user(self) -> float:
        """Minimum subscription price per user so AI attribution covers AI cost."""
        attributed_cost_per_user = self.total_ai_monthly_cost / self.users
        # If 20% of subscription is attributed to AI, then:
        # price × 0.20 >= attributed_cost_per_user → price >= cost/0.20
        return attributed_cost_per_user / self.revenue_attribution_pct

# Example
bea = AIBreakEvenAnalysis(
    users=3_000,
    monthly_token_cost_usd=9_000,
    monthly_ai_infra_usd=800,
    monthly_ai_support_usd=200,
    revenue_attribution_pct=0.20,
)
print(f"Break-even price: ${bea.break_even_price_per_user():.2f}/user/month")
# Break-even price: $16.67/user/month
# If the plan costs $12/user, AI is below break-even
Margin waterfall analysis

A margin waterfall shows how each cost component reduces revenue to arrive at gross margin. Including AI token cost explicitly in the waterfall — rather than burying it in 'infrastructure' — makes the AI cost visible to decision-makers and enables targeted action.
Start with gross revenue per user per month.
Subtract payment processing (direct % of revenue).
Subtract AI token cost (variable; from cost ledger).
Subtract AI infrastructure overhead (semi-fixed).
Subtract other COGS (hosting, data, licensing).
Subtract AI-related support cost.
Arrive at gross margin; compare to target (e.g., 65%).

When the waterfall shows gross margin below target, the AI token cost line is the most actionable lever — it can be reduced through model optimisation, caching, or batching without changing the product's core functionality. Other COGS items are harder to move quickly.
Common mistakes

Attributing 100% of revenue to the AI feature: an AI writing assistant embedded in an existing product does not generate 100% of the product's revenue — over-attribution flatters the feature's margin and leads to under-pricing.

Excluding AI infrastructure overhead from the feature's cost: the gateway, logging, and monitoring costs associated with AI are part of the feature's marginal cost and should appear in the margin model.
Treating AI cost as fixed: AI token cost is variable — it scales with usage. A margin model built on last month's token spend will be wrong when user engagement changes.

Not modelling the interaction effect with support cost: well-designed AI features can reduce inbound support volume, creating a cost saving that partially offsets token cost — include this in the model.

Ignoring usage concentration: if 5% of users drive 50% of token cost, the flat-subscription model is subsidising those heavy users at the expense of margin. Usage-based pricing or soft limits are the right response.
Key takeaways
AI token cost is a COGS line item. Target 10–20% of revenue; above 25% compresses SaaS gross margins to levels that threaten operating viability.
Contribution margin for an AI feature equals attributed revenue minus all incremental AI costs — token cost, AI infrastructure, and AI-related support.
Revenue attribution requires a defensible method: A/B test, cohort analysis, willingness-to-pay survey, or direct pricing of the AI feature.
When AI costs compress margin, the three levers are: reduce token cost (engineering optimisation), reduce usage (quotas), or raise price (product decision).
Break-even analysis sets the floor on subscription pricing — below that price, the AI feature is a margin liability regardless of engagement.
